When the Canaanite kings heard that Israel had crossed the Jordan, their hearts melted with fear. Yet Israel's moment of military triumph is immediately interrupted by something unexpected: circumcision. God does not rush His people into battle. Instead, He calls them to undergo a covenant act that makes them vulnerable—laid up in camp, healing from wounds they've chosen to receive. This is the God who leads, and His leading often looks nothing like what we imagine strength should be. The very generation that refused to enter Canaan forty years earlier has died in the wilderness, their disobedience costing them the promised land. Now their sons—born in exile, never circumcised—must be marked with the sign of Abraham's covenant before they can possess what was sworn to their fathers. God does not pretend the past didn't happen. He acknowledges the weight of it, the lost years, the broken promises of those who came before. Yet He moves forward anyway, raising up a new generation to inherit what the old could not claim.

When Joshua circumcises the nation, something shifts. God declares that He has "rolled away the reproach of Egypt" from them. They are no longer a people marked by slavery and wilderness wandering. The place is renamed Gilgal, a name meaning "to roll away," and that very evening they celebrate the Passover—the feast that commemorates their deliverance from Egypt. The next day, they eat grain from Canaan itself. And then the manna stops. For forty years, God sustained them with bread from heaven in a land that could not feed them. Now they stand in a land that provides, and the miraculous provision ends. This is not abandonment. This is arrival. God is saying: you are home now. You are established. You are no longer dependent on my miraculous daily provision because you now possess the land itself. The old way of life is over.

But before a single stone falls from Jericho's walls, Joshua encounters someone who will define everything that follows. He sees a man with a drawn sword and asks whether this Commander is for Israel or against them. The answer is neither—and everything. This figure is not Israel's ally in the way Joshua imagines. He is not coming to serve Israel's agenda. He is the Commander of the Lord's army, and He stands above every human conflict. When Joshua falls in reverence and asks what he should do, he is told to remove his sandals because the ground is holy. Joshua is standing on God's ground, under God's command, about to witness God's conquest. The battle ahead will not be won by human military strategy. It will be won by obedience to a Commander whose presence makes the earth sacred. Everything Joshua has seen so far—the Jordan opening, the circumcision, the end of manna, the Passover kept in the land—has been preparation for this moment: learning to recognize that he is not the leader here. God is.
